an2.32:0.2: 4. The Chapter on the Equanimous Mind
an2.32:1.1: "I will teach you, *bhikkhus*, the domain of the person of bad character and the domain of the person of good character.
an2.32:1.2: Listen to that, attend to it carefully. I will speak."
an2.32:2.1: "And what, *bhikkhus*, is the domain of the person of bad character?
an2.32:2.2: A person of bad character, *bhikkhus*, is ungrateful and does not acknowledge what has been done for them.
an2.32:2.3: This has been recognized among the virtuous, *bhikkhus*: ingratitude and failure to acknowledge what has been done.
an2.32:2.4: This is entirely the domain of the person of bad character — ingratitude and failure to acknowledge what has been done. (…)
an2.32:2.5: A person of good character, *bhikkhus*, is grateful and acknowledges what has been done for them.
an2.32:2.6: This has been recognized among the virtuous, *bhikkhus*: gratitude and acknowledgment of what has been done.
an2.32:2.7: This is entirely the domain of the person of good character — gratitude and acknowledgment of what has been done."
an2.33:1.1: "There are two, *bhikkhus*, whom I say it is not easy to repay.
an2.33:1.4: Even if one were to carry one's mother on one shoulder and one's father on the other for a hundred years, living a hundred years, and were to tend to them by anointing, massaging, bathing, and rubbing their limbs,
an2.33:1.5: and even if they were to relieve themselves right there on one's shoulders — even so, *bhikkhus*, one would not have done enough for one's parents, nor repaid them.
an2.33:1.6: Even if one were to establish one's parents in sovereignty and lordship over this great earth, so rich in the seven treasures — even so, *bhikkhus*, one would not have done enough for one's parents, nor repaid them.
an2.33:1.8: Parents do much for their children, *bhikkhus* — they nurture them, raise them, and show them this world.
an2.33:1.9: But whoever encourages, settles, and establishes parents who lack faith in the accomplishment of faith, who are immoral in the accomplishment of virtue, who are miserly in the accomplishment of generosity, who are unwise in the accomplishment of wisdom — to that extent, *bhikkhus*, one has done enough for one's parents and repaid them."
an2.34:1.1: Then a certain brahmin approached the *Bhagavā*; having approached, he exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*. Having concluded that cordial exchange … he sat down to one side. Seated to one side, that brahmin said this to the *Bhagavā*:
an2.34:1.2: "What does Master Gotama assert? What does he teach?"
an2.34:1.3: "I am one who asserts doing, brahmin, and one who asserts non-doing."
an2.34:1.4: "But how is Master Gotama both one who asserts doing and one who asserts non-doing?"
an2.34:2.1: "Non-doing, brahmin, is what I assert with regard to misconduct of body, misconduct of speech, and misconduct of mind; with regard to the many kinds of bad, unwholesome *dhammas* — non-doing is what I assert.
an2.34:2.2: Doing, brahmin, is what I assert with regard to good conduct of body, good conduct of speech, and good conduct of mind; with regard to the many kinds of wholesome *dhammas* — doing is what I assert.
an2.34:2.3: That is how I am both one who asserts doing and one who asserts non-doing."
an2.34:3.1: "Excellent, Master Gotama … May Master Gotama consider me a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day forth for as long as life lasts."
an2.35:1.1: Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍika approached the *Bhagavā*; having approached, he paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Seated to one side, the householder Anāthapiṇḍika said this to the *Bhagavā*:
an2.35:1.2: "How many, *bhante*, are worthy of offerings in this world, and where should gifts be given?"
an2.35:1.3: "There are two worthy of offerings in this world, householder —
an2.35:1.4: the trainee and the one beyond training.
an2.35:1.5: These two, householder, are worthy of offerings in this world, and it is here that gifts should be given."
an2.35:2.2: Having said this, the Fortunate One, the Teacher, then said further:
an2.35:3.1: "The trainee and the one beyond training in this world —
an2.35:3.2: they are worthy of offerings from those who sacrifice;
an2.35:3.5: they are the field for those who sacrifice —
an2.35:3.6: a gift given here bears great fruit."
an2.36:1.10: "Yes, friend," those *bhikkhus* replied to the venerable Sāriputta.
an2.36:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika's park.
an2.36:1.3: At that time, the venerable Sāriputta was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in the Eastern Park, in the Mansion of Migāra's Mother.
an2.36:1.4: There, the venerable Sāriputta addressed the *bhikkhus*:
an2.36:1.6: "Friend," those *bhikkhus* replied to the venerable Sāriputta.
an2.36:1.8: "Friends, I will teach you about the person who is inwardly fettered and the person who is outwardly fettered.
an2.36:1.9: Listen to that, attend to it carefully. I will speak."
an2.36:2.1: "And who, friends, is the inwardly fettered person?
an2.36:2.2: Here, friends, a *bhikkhu* is virtuous, restrained by the Pātimokkha, accomplished in conduct and resort, seeing danger in the slightest fault, training by undertaking the training rules.
an2.36:2.3: On the breakup of the body, after death, he is reborn in a certain company of devas.
an2.36:2.4: Having passed away from there, he is a returner, one who comes back to this state of existence.
an2.36:2.5: This one, friends, is called the inwardly fettered person — a returner, one who comes back to this state of existence.
an2.36:3.1: And who, friends, is the outwardly fettered person?
an2.36:3.2: Here, friends, a *bhikkhu* is virtuous, restrained by the Pātimokkha, accomplished in conduct and resort, seeing danger in the slightest fault, training by undertaking the training rules.
an2.36:3.3: He enters and dwells in a certain peaceful liberation of mind [*citta*].
an2.36:3.4: On the breakup of the body, after death, he is reborn in a certain company of devas.
an2.36:3.5: Having passed away from there, he is a non-returner, one who does not come back to this state of existence.
an2.36:3.6: This one, friends, is called the outwardly fettered person — a non-returner, one who does not come back to this state of existence.
an2.36:4.1: Furthermore, friends, a *bhikkhu* is virtuous … training by undertaking the training rules.
an2.36:4.2: He is practicing for disenchantment with, for fading away from, for the cessation of sensual pleasures.
an2.36:4.3: He is practicing for disenchantment with, for fading away from, for the cessation of existence.
an2.36:4.4: He is practicing for the destruction of *taṇhā*.
an2.36:4.5: He is practicing for the destruction of greed.
an2.36:4.6: On the breakup of the body, after death, he is reborn in a certain company of devas.
an2.36:4.7: Having passed away from there, he is a non-returner, one who does not come back to this state of existence.
an2.36:4.8: This one, friends, is called the outwardly fettered person — a non-returner, one who does not come back to this state of existence."
an2.36:5.1: Then a number of *devatās* of equanimous mind approached the *Bhagavā*; having approached, they paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and stood to one side. Standing to one side, those *devatās* said this to the *Bhagavā*:
an2.36:5.2: "This venerable Sāriputta, *bhante*, is teaching the *bhikkhus* at the Eastern Park, in the Mansion of Migāra's Mother, about the inwardly fettered person and the outwardly fettered person.
an2.36:5.4: It would be good, *bhante*, if the *Bhagavā* would approach the venerable Sāriputta out of compassion."
an2.36:5.6: Then the *Bhagavā* — just as a strong man might extend a bent arm or draw in an extended arm — vanished from Jeta's Grove and appeared before the venerable Sāriputta at the Eastern Park, in the Mansion of Migāra's Mother.
an2.36:5.7: The *Bhagavā* sat down on the seat that had been prepared.
an2.36:5.8: The venerable Sāriputta too paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side.
an2.36:5.9: To the venerable Sāriputta seated to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this:
an2.36:6.1: "Here, Sāriputta, a number of *devatās* of equanimous mind approached me; having approached, they paid homage to me and stood to one side. Standing to one side, those *devatās*, Sāriputta, said this to me:
an2.36:6.10: Therefore, Sāriputta, you should train thus:
an2.36:6.11: 'We will have faculties at peace, minds at peace.'
an2.36:6.12: That is how you should train, Sāriputta.
an2.36:6.13: 'For those of you with faculties at peace, Sāriputta, with minds at peace, your bodily action will be peaceful, your verbal action peaceful, your mental action peaceful.
an2.36:6.14: We will offer what is peaceful to our companions in the holy life.'
an2.36:6.15: That is how you should train, Sāriputta.
an2.36:6.16: The wanderers of other sects, Sāriputta, have not heard this exposition of *dhamma*."
an2.36:6.2: 'This venerable Sāriputta, *bhante*, is teaching the *bhikkhus* at the Eastern Park, in the Mansion of Migāra's Mother, about the inwardly fettered person and the outwardly fettered person.
an2.36:6.4: It would be good, *bhante*, if the *Bhagavā* would approach the venerable Sāriputta out of compassion.'
an2.36:6.5: Now those *devatās*, Sāriputta, ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, or sixty of them standing packed to the tip of a needle's point, do not crowd one another.
an2.36:6.6: It might occur to you, Sāriputta:
an2.36:6.7: 'Surely the minds of those *devatās* must have been cultivated in such a way that ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, or sixty of them standing packed to the tip of a needle's point do not crowd one another.'
an2.36:6.8: But that is not how this should be understood, Sāriputta.
an2.36:6.9: It is right here, Sāriputta, that the minds of those *devatās* have been cultivated in such a way that ten, twenty, … do not crowd one another.
an2.37:1.2: at one time the venerable Mahākaccāna was dwelling at Varaṇā, on the bank of the Bhaddasāri.
an2.37:1.3: Then the brahmin Āramaḍaṇḍa approached the venerable Mahākaccāna; having approached, he exchanged greetings with the venerable Mahākaccāna.
an2.37:1.4: Having concluded that cordial and memorable exchange, he sat down to one side. Seated to one side, the brahmin Āramaḍaṇḍa said this to the venerable Mahākaccāna:
an2.37:1.5: "What, Master Kaccāna, is the cause and condition whereby nobles quarrel with nobles, brahmins with brahmins, and householders with householders?"
an2.37:1.6: "It is on account of attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire and lust, brahmin, that nobles quarrel with nobles, brahmins with brahmins, and householders with householders."
an2.37:10.2: Just as one might set upright what has been overturned, or reveal what has been hidden, or point out the way to one who is lost, or hold up a lamp in the darkness so that those with eyes can see forms — in the same way, the *dhamma* has been made clear by Master Kaccāna in many ways.
an2.37:10.3: I go for refuge to that Master Gotama, and to the *dhamma*, and to the *bhikkhu* *saṅgha*.
an2.37:10.4: May Master Kaccāna consider me a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day forth for as long as life lasts."
an2.37:2.1: "And what, Master Kaccāna, is the cause and condition whereby ascetics quarrel with ascetics?"
an2.37:2.2: "It is on account of attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire for views, brahmin, that ascetics quarrel with ascetics."
an2.37:3.1: "But is there anyone in the world, Master Kaccāna, who has gone beyond this attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire and lust, and beyond this attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire for views?"
an2.37:3.2: "There is, brahmin, someone in the world who has gone beyond this attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire and lust, and beyond this attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire for views."
an2.37:4.1: "And who is that person in the world, Master Kaccāna, who has gone beyond this attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire and lust, and beyond this attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire for views?"
an2.37:4.2: "There is, brahmin, in the eastern regions, a city named Sāvatthī.
an2.37:4.3: There the *Bhagavā* now dwells, the *arahant*, the fully enlightened one.
an2.37:4.4: That *Bhagavā*, brahmin, has gone beyond this attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire and lust, and beyond this attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire for views."
an2.37:5.1: When this was said, the brahmin Āramaḍaṇḍa rose from his seat, arranged his upper robe over one shoulder, knelt with his right knee on the ground, raised his joined palms toward the *Bhagavā*, and three times uttered this solemn utterance:
an2.37:6.1: "Homage to that *Bhagavā*, the *arahant*, the fully enlightened one.
an2.37:7.1: Homage to that *Bhagavā*, the *arahant*, the fully enlightened one.
an2.37:8.1: Homage to that *Bhagavā*, the *arahant*, the fully enlightened one.
an2.37:9.1: That *Bhagavā* who has gone beyond this attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire and lust, and beyond this attachment to, entanglement in, bondage to, obsession with, and clinging to desire for views."
an2.38:1.1: At one time the venerable Mahākaccāna was dwelling at Madhurā, in the Gundā Forest.
an2.38:1.2: Then the brahmin Kandarāyana approached the venerable Mahākaccāna; having approached … he sat down to one side. Seated to one side, the brahmin Kandarāyana said this to the venerable Mahākaccāna:
an2.38:1.3: "I have heard this, Master Kaccāna: 'The ascetic Kaccāna does not pay homage to, rise up for, or offer a seat to brahmins who are old, aged, elderly, advanced in years, and come to the last stage of life.'
an2.38:1.5: Indeed Master Kaccāna does not pay homage to, rise up for, or offer a seat to brahmins who are old, aged, elderly, advanced in years, and come to the last stage of life.
an2.38:1.6: This, Master Kaccāna, is not fitting."
an2.38:2.1: "There is, brahmin, what has been declared by that *Bhagavā*, who knows and sees, the *arahant*, the fully enlightened one: a domain of the elder and a domain of the young.
an2.38:2.2: Even if one is old, brahmin — eighty, ninety, or a hundred years old — if he indulges in sensual pleasures, dwells amidst them, is consumed by the fever of sensual pleasures, is tormented by sensual thoughts, and is eager in the pursuit of sensual pleasures,
an2.38:2.3: then that fool does not count as an elder.
an2.38:2.4: Even if one is young, brahmin — a youth with dark hair, endowed with the blessings of youth, in the prime of life —
an2.38:2.5: if he does not indulge in sensual pleasures, does not dwell amidst them, is not consumed by the fever of sensual pleasures, is not tormented by sensual thoughts, and is not eager in the pursuit of sensual pleasures,
an2.38:2.6: then that wise one does count as an elder."
an2.38:3.1: When this was said, the brahmin Kandarāyana rose from his seat, arranged his upper robe over one shoulder, and paid homage at the feet of a hundred young *bhikkhus* with his head:
an2.38:3.2: "You are elders, standing in the domain of the elder.
an2.38:3.3: We are young, standing in the domain of the young."
an2.38:4.1: "Excellent, Master Kaccāna … May Master Kaccāna consider me a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day forth for as long as life lasts."
an2.39:1.1: "At the time, *bhikkhus*, when thieves are strong, kings are weak.
an2.39:1.2: At such a time, *bhikkhus*, it is not easy for a king to travel forth or travel back, or to make tours of inspection in the border regions.
an2.39:1.3: At such a time, it is also not easy for brahmins and householders to travel forth or travel back, or to look after their affairs outside.
an2.39:1.4: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, at the time when bad *bhikkhus* are strong, good *bhikkhus* are weak.
an2.39:1.5: At such a time, *bhikkhus*, good *bhikkhus* fall silent, keeping quiet in the midst of the *saṅgha*, or they remain in the border regions.
an2.39:1.6: This, *bhikkhus*, leads to the harm of many, the unhappiness of many, to the loss, harm, and suffering of many — of gods and humans.
an2.39:2.1: At the time, *bhikkhus*, when kings are strong, thieves are weak.
an2.39:2.2: At such a time, *bhikkhus*, it is easy for a king to travel forth or travel back, or to make tours of inspection in the border regions.
an2.39:2.3: At such a time, it is also easy for brahmins and householders to travel forth or travel back, or to look after their affairs outside.
an2.39:2.4: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, at the time when good *bhikkhus* are strong, bad *bhikkhus* are weak.
an2.39:2.5: At such a time, *bhikkhus*, bad *bhikkhus* fall silent, keeping quiet in the midst of the *saṅgha*, or they go off in whatever direction.
an2.39:2.6: This, *bhikkhus*, leads to the welfare of many, the happiness of many, to the benefit, welfare, and happiness of many — of gods and humans."
an2.40:1.1: "I do not praise wrong practice in either of two, *bhikkhus* — whether a householder or one who has gone forth.
an2.40:1.2: Whether a householder or one gone forth, *bhikkhus*, one who practices wrongly, on account of that wrong practice, does not succeed in the righteous and wholesome *dhamma*.
an2.40:2.1: I praise right practice in either of two, *bhikkhus* — whether a householder or one who has gone forth.
an2.40:2.2: Whether a householder or one gone forth, *bhikkhus*, one who practices rightly, on account of that right practice, succeeds in the righteous and wholesome *dhamma*."
an2.41:1.1: "Those *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhus*, who, by means of wrongly grasped *suttas* with counterfeited phrasing, obstruct both the meaning and the *dhamma* — those *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhus*, practice for the harm of many, for the unhappiness of many, for the loss, harm, and suffering of many — of gods and humans.
an2.41:1.2: Those *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhus*, generate much demerit, and they cause this true *dhamma* to disappear.
an2.41:2.1: Those *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhus*, who, by means of well-grasped *suttas* with counterfeited phrasing, bring both the meaning and the *dhamma* into conformity — those *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhus*, practice for the welfare of many, for the happiness of many, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of many — of gods and humans.
an2.41:2.2: Those *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhus*, generate much merit, and they sustain this true *dhamma*."
an2.41:2.3: The Chapter on the Equanimous Mind, the fourth.
